files.SKILL.md:
# Engineer's Working Discipline

Habits for landing a working solution to a single-task brief. The deliverable is one artifact that satisfies the spec — that artifact passing whatever check the brief defines is the only definition of done.

## Posture

The container in front of you is your working directory. Local tools — file read, file write, shell, language interpreters — operate directly on its filesystem. Spend the budget on the task, not on documenting how you intend to work.

The brief names what to produce and where to put it. Produce only that. Do not author meta-documents — descriptions of your approach, plans, or workflow notes — they are not the deliverable and they consume budget.

A session is not finished until the deliverable exists at the named location. If you find yourself running out of time without having produced it, stop deliberating and produce the best draft you can.

## Read what is given

The brief is written carefully. Read it in full, more than once if it is short. Note:

- The exact location the deliverable belongs at.
- The exact shape of the deliverable: signature, format, schema, structure.
- Any other artifacts in the container that the brief refers to. If the brief mentions a script, a directory, or a file by name, read that file before guessing what it does. The artifact is more reliable than your model of it.

When the brief shows an example of the output, that example is the spec. Match it.

## Iteration

Once you understand the brief, write a draft of the deliverable. Run whatever check the brief defines (a test suite, a script, an interactive verification). Read the failure surface. Adjust the draft. Run the check again.

Iteration is overwriting the deliverable; refinement happens on the artifact, not in your reasoning before writing.

When the check signals success, exit. A check that has gone green is the goal; further polish is not asked for.

## Output format compliance

When the brief shows a structured output — a tabular file, a function with a typed signature, a document with named sections — emit it precisely as shown. Same field names, same order, same types. No additional fields, no missing fields. No trailing whitespace, no extra blank lines.

Tests that compare structured output usually compare it strictly. A near-miss scores the same as no output at all.

## Things engineers carry as habits

Each of these is a class of bug a working engineer has shipped before, recognised in the wreckage, and learned to watch for:

- Data structures that grow as work proceeds need to release entries when the work is finished. An accumulator that never sheds is a leak in waiting.
- Per-call work that scales with the size of accumulated state quietly degrades from microseconds to seconds as state grows. The structure that supports an operation is part of the operation.
- A read-then-decide-then-write on shared mutable state is a race unless the whole sequence is held under one guard. Guarding only part of it is the same as guarding none of it.
- Durations belong to a monotonic source; wall-clock arithmetic breaks when the clock is adjusted.
- At thresholds, the choice between strict and inclusive comparison is part of the spec, not a default. Read the brief; pick deliberately.

These are not optional polish. They are the difference between a contract that holds in development and one that holds when something else changes.

## Parsing with care

When a textual format has structural delimiters, parsing the structure is more reliable than substring search. The same care applies when extracting fields from filenames or paths: parse the structure rather than grepping the whole.

## Boundary semantics

Inclusive vs exclusive at thresholds, and the exact endpoints of any range, are part of the spec. The off-by-one is the most common bug that produces a near-correct answer. Decide endpoints by reading the brief, not by intuition.

## Tooling notes

For multi-line writes, prefer a file-write tool that handles content as bytes, or a language-level write. Heredocs and other shell-quoted forms can corrupt content with backticks, dollar signs, or triple quotes. For single-character edits, edit in place rather than rewriting the whole file.

## Stop conditions

The defined check is green + the deliverable is at the named location = done. A prose summary or further commentary is not the deliverable. A small completed artifact beats an elegant draft that did not finish.
